era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period. Athens is at the height of its imperial power but facing mounting military and financial strain from multiple simultaneous campaigns. The First Peloponnesian War (461–446 BCE) is in its early stages. Pericles is consolidating political power. The Egyptian campaign is in its final stages and will collapse within a few years. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is increasingly challenged by Athens. Egypt is under Persian control with ongoing rebellion. China's Spring and Autumn period continues.

events.title: Cimon's ostracism and political realignment in Athens
events.description: Cimon, the conservative Athenian general and political leader, is ostracized (exiled for ten years) by popular vote. Cimon had dominated Athenian politics for decades, leading the campaigns against Persia and the expansion of the Delian League. His ostracism marks the triumph of the democratic faction led by Pericles and represents a shift in Athenian foreign policy toward more aggressive imperial expansion and less cooperation with Sparta. The ostracism is a watershed moment in Athenian political history.
key_figures: Cimon (Athenian general and political leader, ostracized)
events.certainty_note: Cimon's ostracism is well-attested in later sources. The precise date is debated, with scholars placing it between 461 and 456 BCE. The assignment to 456 BCE is conventional, though some scholars prefer 461 or 459 BCE.

events.title: Athenian naval victory at the Eurymedon River
events.region: Pamphylia, southern coast of Asia Minor
events.description: An Athenian naval force, possibly under Cimon's command before his ostracism, defeats a Persian fleet and army at the Eurymedon River in Pamphylia. The victory is a major triumph for Athenian naval power and represents the culmination of Athenian campaigns against Persia. The victory secures Athenian dominance in the eastern Mediterranean and demonstrates the superiority of Athenian naval tactics. However, the battle's date is highly disputed among scholars.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Eurymedon is attested in later sources, but its date is highly disputed. Scholars place it anywhere from 469 to 450 BCE, with 456 BCE being one conventional dating. Some scholars argue it occurred in 466 or 465 BCE. The battle may be legendary or conflated with other naval victories.
cross_references: 460-001 (broader context of Athenian campaigns against Persia)

events.title: Athenian Long Walls construction continues
events.description: Construction of the Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus continues. The project is a major engineering undertaking and reflects Athenian confidence in its naval dominance and wealth. The walls are designed to protect the city's supply lines and ensure that Athens can maintain its naval power even if surrounded by land forces.
key_figures: Athenian architects and engineers (names not preserved)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.107
Archaeological evidence from the Long Walls

events.certainty_note: The construction of the Long Walls is well-attested in literary sources and archaeological evidence. The project was completed in stages over several decades, so the assignment to 456 BCE represents one phase of construction.

events.title: Egyptian campaign collapses; Athenian forces defeated
events.description: The Athenian expedition to Egypt, which began in 460 BCE, faces its final collapse. Persian forces, under the command of Artaxerxes I, crush the Egyptian rebellion and expel the Athenian forces. The campaign results in the loss of much of the Athenian fleet and represents a major strategic defeat for Athens. The failure of the Egyptian campaign marks the end of Athenian attempts to challenge Persian power in the eastern Mediterranean and forces Athens to focus on consolidating its power in the Aegean.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.104, 109

events.certainty_note: The collapse of the Egyptian campaign is well-attested in later sources. Thucydides places it after the First Peloponnesian War has begun but does not provide a precise date. Scholars variously date the final collapse to 454, 453, or 452 BCE, with 454 BCE being most common. The assignment to 456 BCE is speculative.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Cimon's ostracism is highly disputed. Scholars place it anywhere from 461 to 456 BCE, with no scholarly consensus. Some scholars argue it occurred in 461 BCE, others in 459 or 456 BCE. The Battle of the Eurymedon is even more disputed; some scholars place it in the 460s BCE, others in the 450s, and some argue it may be legendary or conflated with other battles. The collapse of the Egyptian campaign is variously dated to 454, 453, or 452 BCE, not 456 BCE. The significance of these events is accepted, but their precise dating is highly uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 456 BCE is sparse and largely reconstructed from Thucydides' summary narrative and later compilations. No contemporary Athenian inscriptions or documents are known to survive for this specific year. The Egyptian perspective is almost entirely absent from Greek sources. The dating of events in this period is conventional and subject to significant scholarly debate. The sources are biased toward Athenian perspectives and may overstate Athenian military successes or misdate them.

graph_edges.note: Cimon's ostracism follows from his political opposition to the democratic faction
The Eurymedon victory (if dated to 456) contributes to the eventual Peace of Callias
The Egyptian campaign's collapse occurs around this time period
